summary: This notebook demonstrates a hybrid image forgery detection pipeline that combines a frozen DINOv2 vision transformer encoder with a custom lightweight CNN decoder for pixel-level segmentation. It focuses on inference, threshold tuning, and post-processing rather than training from scratch. The author implements test-time augmentation and an adaptive masking strategy that combines gradient magnitude with local statistics to refine forged region predictions. A grid search optimizes area and mean intensity thresholds to maximize validation F1 score. Readers should learn how to effectively deploy pretrained self-supervised models for forensic segmentation and tune post-processing rules for robust binary decisions.
competition_flow: Loads train/test images and masks, extracts DINOv2 features through a frozen encoder and CNN decoder, applies TTA and adaptive gradient-based masking, optimizes decision thresholds via grid search, and generates an RLE-encoded submission CSV.
data_reading: Images are loaded via PIL.Image.open and converted to RGB
Masks are loaded from .npy files using np.load, with 3D masks reduced by taking the maximum over the channel axis
Paths are constructed using pathlib and os
data_processing: Images are resized to 518x518 and normalized to [0,1]
Masks are resized with nearest-neighbor interpolation
Test-time augmentation applies horizontal and vertical flips averaged during inference
Post-processing uses Sobel gradient enhancement, Gaussian blurring, adaptive thresholding (mean + 0.3*std), and morphological closing/opening operations
models: DinoTinyDecoder (custom CNN with Conv2d, ReLU, Dropout2d, bilinear upsampling)
cv_strategy: Holdout split (80% train, 20% validation) via train_test_split with random_state=42
ensembling: Test-time augmentation (TTA) averages predictions from the original image, horizontally flipped input, and vertically flipped input; post-processing combines gradient-enhanced probability maps with adaptive statistical thresholding and morphological refinement.
insights: Freezing a large self-supervised vision transformer and attaching a lightweight CNN decoder is an efficient way to adapt pre-trained representations for forensic segmentation tasks.
Gradient magnitude enhancement significantly improves mask boundary precision compared to raw probability maps.
Adaptive thresholding based on local statistics (mean + 0.3*std) is more robust than fixed global thresholds for forgery detection.
